A Bayesian VAR Suite for Forecasting the Australian Economy

Block-exogenous small-open-economy BVARs, pooled density forecasts, 1–12 quarters ahead

Published

June 13, 2026

1. What this is

This report documents a suite of Bayesian VARs for forecasting the key Australian macroeconomic aggregates 1–12 quarters ahead, combined into a single pooled density forecast. Australia is modelled as a small open economy: every VAR member partitions the system into a foreign block (world activity, commodity prices, the foreign policy rate) and a domestic block, and block exogeneity forbids domestic variables from feeding back into the foreign block (Cushman & Zha 1997; Zha 1999), at lags and contemporaneously. This is imposed in estimation (not just on impulse responses) and verified numerically (§6).

Data source for this run: synthetic. All results below are produced from the synthetic block-exogenous DGP, which exists so that the pipeline runs end-to-end offline; the relative model rankings are informative about the machinery, not about Australia. Re-run with data.source: real for substantive results.

2. Data and transformations

The transform_spec table is the single source of truth mapping each series to its transform, Minnesota own-lag prior mean (delta), block, and steady-state prior anchor (used by the Villani members).

variable label block transform delta ss_mean ss_sd
f_act World activity (US IP proxy) foreign dlog 0 0.45 0.25
f_comm Commodity prices (RBA ICP, SDR) foreign dlog 0 0.00 1.00
f_rate Foreign policy rate (US fed funds) foreign level 1 2.50 1.00
gdp_growth Real GDP growth (qtr %) domestic dlog 0 0.70 0.20
cpi_inflation Trimmed-mean CPI inflation (qtr %) domestic dlog 0 0.62 0.15
unemp_rate Unemployment rate (%) domestic level 1 4.50 0.75
cash_rate Cash rate (%) domestic level 1 3.50 1.00
rtwi Real TWI (100*log) domestic loglevel 1 NA 10.00
tot Terms of trade (100*log) domestic loglevel 1 NA 10.00
wpi_growth Wage price index growth (qtr %) domestic dlog 0 0.80 0.20
emp_growth Employment growth (qtr %) domestic dlog 0 0.45 0.20
cons_growth Household consumption growth (qtr %) domestic dlog 0 0.70 0.20
bond10y 10y AGS yield (%) domestic level 1 4.00 1.00

Growth-type series enter as 100·Δlog with delta = 0; rates and 100·log levels enter with delta = 1 plus sum-of-coefficients and dummy-initial-observation priors in selected members to discipline the I(1) behaviour at long horizons.

3. The suite

Seven block-exogenous VARs spanning prior family (Minnesota vs steady-state), size (8-variable SOE core vs 13-variable medium), tightness (GLP-style marginal-likelihood-selected λ vs fixed tight/loose), lag length (2/4/5) and volatility (constant vs stochastic), plus four univariate anchors (random walk, AR(4), UCSV, unconditional mean). All members are estimated recursively at every forecast origin and produce predictive draws for every target variable and horizon, so they pool cleanly.

4. Evaluation design

Pseudo-real-time expanding-window evaluation: at each origin t every member sees only data through t — including the data-driven tightness selection, which is re-run inside each origin. Combination weights at t use only scores whose realization was observable at t (s + h ≤ t). Both properties are enforced structurally and verified by automated tests (§6). Densities are scored with the log score and CRPS (scoringRules), points with RMSE, and calibration with PIT histograms, all by horizon, for quarterly and year-ended transforms.

4½. The COVID quarters

Three quarters of 2020 are 5–30σ events. Following Lenza & Primiceri (2022), the constant-volatility members scale up the residual covariance in the configured COVID quarters — a free scale factor per quarter, estimated by marginal likelihood recursively at each forecast origin, with the excess volatility decaying geometrically afterwards — implemented as GLS row weighting, with the decaying scale path carried into the predictive shocks at COVID-era origins. The SV members instead use t-distributed errors (the Carriero–Clark–Marcellino–Mertens SV-t specification), so the spike is absorbed by the transitory scale mixture rather than the persistent volatility process. The Minnesota σ-calibration and the data-driven tightness selection run on the same weighted data (Hartwig 2024). COVID observations remain in the forecast jump-off — only their likelihood contribution is down-weighted. A dummy option (≈ dropping the quarters, Schorfheide–Song 2024) is available for comparison; its known cost is unrealistically tight predictive bands at pandemic-era origins. See DECISIONS.md D17.

Because a handful of extreme realizations dominate mean log scores, the tables below are complemented by output/tables/scores_by_horizon_excovid.csv, which excludes the COVID realization quarters from scoring.

5.4 Calibration

PIT histograms by member (h = 1 and h = 4): a calibrated density forecast has uniform PITs. Hump-shaped histograms indicate over-dispersion, U-shapes under-dispersion.

6. Diagnostics

Section-9 self-checks, enforced as a hard gate in the pipeline:

member ess_min stable_share_min block_exog_max converged_all sanity_all no_lookahead reproducible
small_minn 920.0020 0.9967 0 TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE
small_ss 851.8419 1.0000 0 TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE
small_sv 222.0339 1.0000 0 TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE
small_loose_p5 982.6767 0.9900 0 TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE
medium_minn 214.2581 1.0000 0 TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE
medium_conj 1000.0000 0.9950 0 TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE
small_tight 1000.0000 1.0000 0 TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE
rw Inf 1.0000 0 TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE
ar4 Inf 1.0000 0 TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE
ucsv 26.1422 1.0000 0 TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE
ucmean Inf 1.0000 0 TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE
  • Block exogeneity binds: max |posterior-mean coefficient of a domestic lag inside a foreign equation| across all members and origins is 1e-05 (engines with structural zeros report exactly 0).
  • No look-ahead: corrupting all post-origin data leaves every forecast draw bit-identical.
  • Reproducibility: re-running an origin with the same master seed gives identical draws.

7. Honest limitations

  • Final-vintage data. The evaluation is pseudo-real-time on (for real runs) final data; genuine real-time vintages would change GDP-related rankings.
  • Combination gains are modest by design. Equal weights are hard to beat (the combination puzzle); the payoff we target is calibration and robustness, not large point-forecast gains, and the tables should be read that way — where a single member beats the pools at a given horizon, the honest summary is that the pool buys insurance, not accuracy.
  • Conditional forecasts use hard substitution, not Waggoner–Zha shock redistribution; they are a convenience hook, not a policy-scenario tool.
  • The synthetic DGP is block-exogenous and Gaussian-with-outliers; it validates machinery (recovery of the block structure, calibration under a known truth), not Australian dynamics.
